explicit_text: On the day of the attacks, I was heading to Ellis Island after hearing about the first plane hitting the North Tower. We evacuated people from Liberty Island to Ellis, and Ellis became a triage and staging area. After the second tower collapsed, I brought people back to Ellis Island because Manhattan was filled with smoke. Post-attack, Ellis Island was secured with bomb-sniffing dogs and car inspections, and our boats were kept there instead of Manhattan.
implicit_text: On the day of the attacks, I was heading to the immigration station after hearing about the first plane hitting the North Tower. We evacuated people from Liberty Island to the immigration station, and it became a triage and staging area. After the second tower collapsed, I brought people back there because Manhattan was filled with smoke. Post-attack, the island was secured with bomb-sniffing dogs and car inspections, and our boats were kept there instead of Manhattan.

explicit_text: Larry was my deckhand that morning when I was doing an oil change at the Marine Inspection Office. He was the one who first told me that a plane had hit one of the buildings. His alert was the start of everything for me that day, as I came upstairs and saw the smoke over the buildings.

explicit_text: I was at the Marine Inspection Office in Manhattan near the Staten Island Ferry Terminal when the attacks began. I was downstairs doing an oil change on a generator when Larry told me a plane hit a building. From the MIO, I could see smoke over the buildings but not the Trade Center itself until I backed out and headed north.

explicit_text: I witnessed the attacks on the World Trade Center firsthand from the water. I first saw smoke coming from the North Tower around 9 a.m., and then I saw the second plane hit the South Tower from the corner of my eye. The collapse of the towers sent smoke rolling down Manhattan, and the area around the Financial Center was covered in debris.

explicit_text: I saw the second plane hit the South Tower from the corner of my eye while I was heading north. It was shocking and eerie because I didn’t realize we were under attack until that moment. Later, the South Tower collapsed, sending smoke rolling down Manhattan.
implicit_text: I saw the second plane hit the southern of the twin towers from the corner of my eye while I was heading north. It was shocking and eerie because I didn’t realize we were under attack until that moment. Later, the southern tower collapsed, sending smoke rolling down Manhattan.

explicit_text: Manhattan was the central location of the attacks, and I was navigating boats around its waterfront that day. After the towers collapsed, the smoke rolled down the streets, making it impossible to drop people off safely. I also drove through Manhattan later that evening and crossed the Brooklyn Bridge, witnessing people walking across it amid the chaos.

explicit_text: Jim Watkins was with me in the wheelhouse on the boat when we were heading back into Manhattan after evacuating people. He is the budget analyst at Ellis Island. We were about three-quarters of the way in when the second tower collapsed, and we had to turn back because of the smoke.

explicit_text: Pete O'Dougherty is the chief of maintenance and my boss. After the towers collapsed, he coordinated switching boats from Liberty IV to Liberty II to better handle potential medical evacuations. He also was with us later that evening when we dropped people off near the Brooklyn Bridge and the Circle Line at 42nd Street.

explicit_text: When we arrived in Manhattan after the towers collapsed, Battery Park City was packed with every boat imaginable, all picking up people and taking them to safety. It was a major hub for evacuation efforts, and we were told to stand by because the area was overwhelmed with rescue vessels.

explicit_text: The NYPD was controlling access to critical areas like the Brooklyn Bridge after the attacks. When I tried to cross the Brooklyn Bridge, a lieutenant from the NYPD stopped me but then allowed me through after I identified myself. They had stanchions set up and were restricting all but essential traffic.

explicit_text: I crossed the Brooklyn Bridge alone in my car that evening after the attacks, which felt eerie because I was the only vehicle allowed. On the bridge, I saw many people walking across on foot, staggering and straggling as they evacuated the city. The NYPD had blocked the bridge but made an exception for me after I explained who I was.

explicit_text: I live in Long Island, and after the attacks, all westbound traffic into Manhattan was closed, so I couldn’t get home for a few days. When I finally could return to work, I had to take the Verrazzano-Narrows Bridge and Bayonne Bridge, paying multiple tolls just to get to the city. It was a tough commute for several weeks.

explicit_text: Liberty II is one of the boats we use, mainly for garbage hauling because it has a wide-open deck. It has a single 671 engine and is not used for passenger transport except in emergencies. After the attacks, we switched to using Liberty II for potential medical evacuations because of its open deck space.

explicit_text: Liberty III serves mostly as a backup to Liberty IV and sometimes hauls garbage, though not as much as Liberty II. During the attacks, my boss Mike Kartanowicz was commandeered from the Fire Department and used Liberty III to shuttle firemen back and forth to the North Shore Marina. It was an important support vessel during the emergency.

explicit_text: Liberty IV is our main boat, 64 feet long with 1400 horsepower, used primarily to transport staff and VIPs between Ellis and Liberty Islands. On the day of the attacks, I was operating Liberty IV when evacuations began. It holds 80 people and was critical in moving people quickly during the crisis.

explicit_text: On September 11, 2001, I was at the Marine Inspection Office when the first plane hit the North Tower. I witnessed the second plane hit the South Tower from the water and participated in evacuating people from Liberty and Ellis Islands. The collapse of the towers sent smoke rolling through Manhattan, and the city was locked down with heavy security. I helped transport evacuees and emergency personnel throughout the day and experienced the disruption to commuting and daily life afterward.
